name: Temple of Artemis
description: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus, also known less precisely as the Artemision, was a Greek temple dedicated to the goddess Artemis. It was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Located in Ephesus, near present-day Selçuk in Turkey, the temple underwent several reconstructions, each grander than the last. The most famous version, completed after a fire in 356 BC purportedly set by Herostratus, was a monumental structure adorned with impressive sculptures and colossal columns. It served not only as a place of worship but also as a significant economic and cultural hub, attracting pilgrims and merchants from across the ancient world. The temple was eventually destroyed by the Goths in 262 AD, and only fragments remain today, offering a glimpse into its former splendor and the devotion it inspired.
subtitle: Ancient Greek temple dedicated to Artemis.
significance: A major religious center and one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, symbolizing wealth, power, and devotion to Artemis.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Artemis
keyMyths.description: Artemis, the goddess of the hunt, wilderness, wild animals, the Moon, and archery, was born on the island of Ortygia to Leto, while she was fleeing from the wrath of Hera. Leto found refuge on the island and gave birth to Artemis, who then assisted in the birth of her twin brother, Apollo. This divine birth story solidified Artemis's connection to nature and her role as a protector of wild places.

keyMyths.title: The Burning of the Temple
keyMyths.description: In 356 BC, Herostratus, a man seeking fame, set fire to the Temple of Artemis. He hoped to achieve notoriety through this act of destruction. Although he was executed and his name was banned from being spoken, his act remains infamous, highlighting the temple's vulnerability and the lengths to which some would go for recognition, even through destruction.

keyMyths.title: Iphigenia and Artemis
keyMyths.description: Before the Achaean fleet could sail to Troy, Artemis demanded the sacrifice of Iphigenia, daughter of Agamemnon. Agamemnon had offended the goddess, and the winds would not blow until the sacrifice was made. In some versions of the story, Artemis took pity on Iphigenia at the last moment and replaced her with a deer, whisking Iphigenia away to Tauris to serve as a priestess in her temple.

sources.author: Strabo
sources.description: Provides geographical and historical context for Ephesus and the Temple of Artemis.
sources.text: Provides geographical and historical context for Ephesus and the Temple of Artemis.
